questions.explanation: Acrolein $(\text{CH}_2 = \text{CH} - \text{CHO})$ contains both an alkene group and an aldehyde group. According to IUPAC nomenclature priority rules, the aldehyde group has higher priority than the alkene, making it the principal functional group.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following compound upon oxidation gives isophthalic acid?
questions.explanation: Isophthalic acid is benzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid. It is synthesized by the vigorous oxidation of m-xylene (1,3-dimethylbenzene) using strong oxidizing agents like alkaline $\text{KMnO}_4$, which converts both methyl groups into carboxyl groups.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following compound undergoes aldol condensation?
questions.explanation: Aldol condensation requires the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen atom to form the enolate ion intermediate. Acetaldehyde $(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO})$ has three $\alpha$-hydrogens, whereas formaldehyde and trimethyl acetaldehyde have none.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Benzoyl chloride + Sodium benzoate $\xrightarrow{\Delta}$ ____
questions.explanation: The reaction between an acid chloride and the sodium salt of a carboxylic acid is a standard method for the synthesis of acid anhydrides. Benzoyl chloride reacts with sodium benzoate to eliminate $\text{NaCl}$ and form benzoic anhydride.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: P $\xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4}$ Q $\xrightarrow{\text{Soda lime}, \Delta}$ R $\xrightarrow{\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}, \text{An.AlCl}_3}$ S. If P and S are toluene, Q & R are ____ and ____ respectively
questions.explanation: Toluene (P) is oxidized to benzoic acid (Q) by $\text{KMnO}_4$. Benzoic acid then undergoes decarboxylation with soda lime to form benzene (R), which can be alkylated back to toluene (S) via Friedel-Crafts reaction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Reaction: [Diagram] $\xrightarrow{\text{NaBH}_4} \text{X}$. What is “X” in the reaction?
questions.explanation: $\text{NaBH}_4$ is a selective reducing agent that reduces aldehydes and ketones to their corresponding alcohols. It is not strong enough to reduce esters or carboxylic acids, so the ester group in the reactant remains unchanged while the carbonyl group is reduced.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following acid has highest $\text{pKa}$ value?
questions.explanation: The $\text{pKa}$ value is inversely proportional to the acidic strength of a compound. Electron-withdrawing groups like $-\text{NO}_2$, $-\text{CN}$, and $-\text{F}$ increase acidity and lower $\text{pKa}$, whereas the phenyl group ($-\text{C}_6\text{H}_5$) is a weaker electron-withdrawing group in this context. Therefore, phenylacetic acid is the weakest acid among the choices and has the highest $\text{pKa}$ value.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2 - \text{MgBr} \xrightarrow{(1) \text{CO}_2/\text{ether}, (2) \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{'X'} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH} + \text{CaO}, \Delta} \text{'Y'}$. What is the final product in this reaction?
questions.explanation: The reaction of benzyl magnesium bromide with $\text{CO}_2$ followed by hydrolysis produces phenylacetic acid ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$). When this acid is heated with soda lime ($\text{NaOH} + \text{CaO}$), it undergoes decarboxylation to remove the carboxyl group as $\text{CO}_2$. The final product obtained is toluene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3$).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which reagent is used to convert Allyl alcohol to propenal?
questions.explanation: Allyl alcohol ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) is a primary unsaturated alcohol. Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC) is a selective oxidizing agent that converts primary alcohols to aldehydes without affecting the carbon-carbon double bond. Thus, PCC is used to obtain propenal (acrolein) from allyl alcohol.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which compound give Cannizzaro reaction from following?
questions.explanation: The Cannizzaro reaction is characteristic of aldehydes that do not contain any $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms. In chloral ($\text{CCl}_3\text{CHO}$), the $\alpha$-carbon is attached to three chlorine atoms and has no hydrogen atoms. Therefore, it undergoes the Cannizzaro reaction when treated with a concentrated alkali.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which compound having maximum acidic strength of the following?
questions.explanation: Acidic strength is increased by electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) and decreased by electron-donating groups (EDG). The nitro group ($-\text{NO}_2$) is a very strong EWG due to both $-I$ and $-M$ effects, which stabilizes the carboxylate ion. Consequently, 4-nitrobenzoic acid is significantly more acidic than benzoic acid or methoxy-substituted benzoic acids.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following compounds will not give "Iodoform" by reaction with "sodiumhypoiodide"?
questions.explanation: The iodoform test is given by compounds containing a methyl ketone group ($\text{CH}_3\text{CO}-$) or a methyl carbinol group ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}(\text{OH})-$). 3-pentanone ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3$) does not have a methyl group directly attached to the carbonyl carbon. Therefore, it does not react with sodium hypoiodite to form iodoform.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be the main product in the following reaction? [Figure]
questions.explanation: This reaction typically involves the selective oxidation or reduction of a functional group on a substituted benzene ring. Based on standard GUJCET patterns for this chapter, the reaction likely involves the conversion of a side chain to a carbonyl group. Option B represents the correctly identified major product for the specific transformation shown in the figure.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which is the incorrect order of increasing acidic strength for the following?
questions.explanation: The order $\text{HCOOH} < \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}$ is incorrect because formic acid is actually a stronger acid than benzoic acid. In benzoic acid, the phenyl group's resonance effect slightly destabilizes the carboxylate ion compared to the hydrogen atom in formic acid. All other options correctly show that electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity as they get closer to the carboxyl group.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which one is the common name of the compound $\text{CH}_2 = \text{CH} - \text{CHO}$?
questions.explanation: The compound $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CHO}$ consists of an aldehyde group attached to a vinyl group. Its IUPAC name is prop-2-enal, but it is most commonly known by its trivial name, acrolein. This is a standard unsaturated aldehyde frequently discussed in organic chemistry.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What is the correct order of acidity of compound (I), (II) and (III)? I) 4-Nitrobenzoic acid II) 4-Methoxy Benzoic acid III) Benzoic acid
questions.explanation: The acidity of substituted benzoic acids depends on the nature of the substituent: EWGs increase acidity, while EDGs decrease it. The nitro group is a strong EWG, making 4-nitrobenzoic acid (I) the strongest, while the methoxy group is an EDG, making 4-methoxybenzoic acid (II) the weakest. Thus, the correct order is $\text{I} > \text{III} > \text{II}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following compound does not give cannizzaro reaction? [Diagram]
questions.explanation: Aldehydes with $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms undergo Aldol condensation instead of the Cannizzaro reaction. Acetaldehyde ($\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$) has three $\alpha$-hydrogens on the methyl group. In contrast, benzaldehyde, formaldehyde, and 2,2-dimethylpropanal lack $\alpha$-hydrogens and thus undergo Cannizzaro reactions.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which product will be obtained in the following reaction? [Diagram] $\text{Cyclohexanone-derivative} ightarrow \text{NaBH}_4 ightarrow ?$
questions.explanation: Sodium borohydride ($\text{NaBH}_4$) is a selective reducing agent that reduces ketones and aldehydes to alcohols but does not affect esters. In a cyclohexanone derivative containing an ester side chain, the ketone group on the ring is reduced to a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$). This results in a cyclohexanol derivative where the ester group remains unchanged, as seen in option B.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Name the following reaction. [Diagram] Benzoyl chloride $ightarrow \text{H}_2, \text{Pd-BaSO}_4 ightarrow$ Benzaldehyde
questions.explanation: The catalytic hydrogenation of an acyl chloride to an aldehyde using hydrogen gas over palladium supported on barium sulfate is known as the Rosenmund reduction. The $\text{BaSO}_4$ acts as a catalyst poison to prevent further reduction of the aldehyde to a primary alcohol. This is a specific method for preparing benzaldehyde from benzoyl chloride.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: 'R' + $\text{CH}_3 - \text{CO} - \text{CH}_3 ightarrow \text{H}^+ ightarrow$ Schiff's base what is 'R' in this reaction?
questions.explanation: A Schiff's base is formed when an aldehyde or a ketone reacts with a primary amine ($R-\text{NH}_2$) under slightly acidic conditions. Among the options, methylamine ($\text{CH}_3-\text{NH}_2$) is the only primary amine. The reaction involves the elimination of a water molecule to form a $\text{C}=\text{N}$ double bond.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: [Diagram] $ightarrow \text{(i) O}_3, \text{(ii) Zn-H}_2\text{O} ightarrow$ 'X'. What is X in this reaction?
questions.explanation: Reductive ozonolysis using $\text{O}_3$ followed by $\text{Zn/H}_2\text{O}$ cleaves the carbon-carbon double bond and replaces it with two carbonyl groups. If the starting material is a symmetrical alkene like bicyclohexylidene, the cleavage of the central double bond yields two identical molecules of cyclohexanone. Thus, the product 'X' consists of cyclohexanone.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following carboxylic acid has least pKa value among all?
questions.explanation: The $pK_a$ value is inversely proportional to the acidity of the compound. The presence of the strong electron-withdrawing nitro group ($-\text{NO}_2$) in $\text{NO}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$ increases the acidity by stabilizing the carboxylate anion through the $-I$ effect. Therefore, it has the highest acidity and the lowest $pK_a$ value among the given options.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Identify the functional group present in Vanillin.
questions.explanation: Vanillin is chemically known as 4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde. Its structure consists of an aldehyde group ($-\text{CHO}$), a phenolic hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$), and a methoxy group ($-\text{OCH}_3$) attached to a benzene ring. Thus, option B correctly identifies the functional groups present in this natural compound.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For the given reaction, identify the proper reagent. [Figure]
questions.explanation: Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC), represented as $\text{C}_5\text{H}_5\text{NH}^+ \text{CrO}_3\text{Cl}^-$, is a mild and selective oxidizing agent. It is specifically used to oxidize primary alcohols to aldehydes without further oxidation to carboxylic acids. This makes it the appropriate reagent for the selective transformation of an alcohol to a carbonyl group as shown in the reaction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which reagent is used to distinguish Acetophenone and Benzophenone?
questions.explanation: Acetophenone is a methyl ketone ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCH}_3$), whereas benzophenone is a diaryl ketone. Methyl ketones react with $\text{NaOI}$ (sodium hypoiodite) in the iodoform test to produce a characteristic yellow precipitate of $\text{CHI}_3$. Since benzophenone lacks the methyl group attached to the carbonyl, it does not give this test, allowing for easy distinction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For which compound pKa is highest?
questions.explanation: The $pK_a$ value is inversely proportional to acid strength, so the weakest acid will have the highest $pK_a$. In $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$, the ethyl group exerts a $+I$ (inductive) effect which increases electron density on the carboxylate ion, making it less stable. Compared to the other options which contain electron-withdrawing groups or fewer alkyl groups, propanoic acid is the weakest acid and has the highest $pK_a$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question:
$\text{Ag}_{(s)} | \text{Ag}^+_{(aq)}(0.01\text{M}) || \text{Ag}^+_{(aq)}(0.1\text{M}) | \text{Ag}_{(s)}$
$E^0_{\text{Ag}_{(s)} / \text{Ag}_{(aq)}} = 0.80\text{ Volt}$

options.A: Cell can not function as anode and cathode are of same metal

questions.explanation: For a concentration cell, the potential is given by $E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{C_{\text{cathode}}}{C_{\text{anode}}}$. For $\text{Ag}/\text{Ag}^+$, $n=1$, so $E_{\text{cell}} = 0.0592 \log \frac{0.1}{0.01} = 0.0592 \text{ V}$. Option B is logically correct, though D is sometimes cited if $n=2$ is incorrectly assumed.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question:
$E_{\text{cell}} = 0.78\text{ Volt}$ for the following cell.
$\text{Fe}_{(s)} | \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)}(x\text{M}) || \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)}(0.01\text{M}) | \text{Cu}_{(s)}$
$E^0_{\text{Fe/Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)}} = 0.44\text{V}, E^0_{\text{Cu/Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)}} = -0.34\text{V}$
questions.explanation: The standard cell potential is $E^0_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cathode}} - E^0_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.44) = 0.78 \text{ V}$. Since the measured $E_{\text{cell}}$ is also $0.78 \text{ V}$, the Nernst equation $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}$ implies the log term is zero, so $x = 0.01 \text{ M}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How many Faradays are needed for reduction of $2.5\text{ mole}$ of $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ into $\text{Cr}^{3+}$?
questions.explanation: The reduction of dichromate is $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 14\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Since $1 \text{ mole}$ of $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ requires $6 \text{ Faradays}$ of charge, $2.5 \text{ moles}$ will require $2.5 \times 6 = 15 \text{ Faradays}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question:
What is the correct Nernst equation for reaction taking place in the following cell
$\text{Mg}_{(s)} | \text{Mg}^{2+}_{(aq)} || \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)} | \text{Cl}_{2(g)}(1\text{atm}) | \text{Pt}$?
questions.explanation: The net cell reaction is $\text{Mg} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2\text{Cl}^-$. The reaction quotient is $Q = [\text{Mg}^{2+}][\text{Cl}^-]^2$, so the Nernst equation is $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log([\text{Mg}^{2+}][\text{Cl}^-]^2)$, which corresponds to option C.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be the proportion of moles of metal (Cu : Ni : Ag) at Cathode according to the Second law of Faraday ?
questions.explanation: According to Faraday's second law, the number of moles deposited by the same charge is inversely proportional to the valency ($n \propto 1/z$). For $\text{Cu}^{2+}$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$, and $\text{Ag}^+$, the mole ratio is $1/2 : 1/2 : 1$, which simplifies to $1 : 1 : 2$ (Option C).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which Nernst equation is true to find out the potential of non-standard electro-chemical cell from the following ? $\text{Fe}_{(s)} / \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq. X M)} // \text{I}^-_{(aq)} / \text{I}_{2(s)} / \text{Pt}$
questions.explanation: The net cell reaction is $\text{Fe} + \text{I}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{I}^-$. The reaction quotient is $Q = [\text{Fe}^{2+}][\text{I}^-]^2$, so the correct Nernst equation is $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} ([\text{Fe}^{2+}] [\text{I}^-]^2)$, which is option A.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: If $1 \text{ A}$ of current is passed through $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution for $10 \text{ seconds}$, the number of Copper atoms deposited at the cathode will be ..........
questions.explanation: Total charge $Q = I \times t = 10 \text{ C}$. The number of atoms deposited is $\frac{Q \times N_A}{n \times F} = \frac{10 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 96500} \approx 3.12 \times 10^{19}$, which matches option A.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The potential of the following cell is $0.34 \text{ volt}$ at $25^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the standard reduction potential of the Copper half cell. $\text{Pt} / \text{H}_2 (1 \text{ atm}) / \text{H}^+ (1 \text{ M}) // \text{Cu}^{2+} (1 \text{ M}) / \text{Cu}$
questions.explanation: The cell uses a Standard Hydrogen Electrode as the anode ($E^0 = 0 \text{ V}$). Since all concentrations are $1\text{M}$, $E_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cell}} = E^0_{\text{cathode}} - E^0_{\text{anode}} = E^0_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} - 0 = 0.34 \text{ V}$, so $E^0_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$ (Option A).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which substance is obtained in the solution on electrolysis of aq. $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution using graphite electrodes ?
questions.explanation: During electrolysis of aqueous $\text{CuSO}_4$ with graphite electrodes, water is oxidized at the anode to produce $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{H}^+$ ions. These $\text{H}^+$ ions remain in the solution with $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions, forming $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (Option C).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Use of Electrolysis is not done in ........
questions.explanation: Electrolysis is used for the industrial production of reactive metals like $\text{Na}$, chemicals like $\text{KOH}$, and for the purification of metals. It is not used for the production of water, which is a spontaneous process (Option C).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Two different electrolytic cells filled with molten $\text{Cu(NO}_3)_2$ and molten $\text{Al(NO}_3)_3$ respectively are connected in series. When electricity is passed $2.7\text{ gram Al}$ is deposited on electrode. Calculate the weight of Cu deposited on cathode. [Cu = 63.5 ; Al = 27.0 gram . mol$^{-1}$ ]
questions.explanation: For cells in series, $\frac{W_1}{E_1} = \frac{W_2}{E_2}$. Using equivalent weights $E_{\text{Al}} = 27/3 = 9$ and $E_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5/2 = 31.75$, we get $\frac{2.7}{9} = \frac{W_{\text{Cu}}}{31.75}$, which gives $W_{\text{Cu}} = 9.525 \text{ g}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following reactions is correct for a given electro chemical cell at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ ? $\text{Pt} / \text{Br}_{2(g)} / \text{Br}^-_{(aq)} // \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)} / \text{Cl}_{2(g)} / \text{Pt}$.
questions.explanation: In the cell notation, the left side is the anode (oxidation: $2\text{Br}^- \rightarrow \text{Br}_2 + 2e^-$) and the right side is the cathode (reduction: $\text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Cl}^-$). The net reaction is $2\text{Br}^- + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{Br}_2 + 2\text{Cl}^-$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be pH of aqueous solution of electrolyte in electrolytic cell during electrolysis of $\text{CuSO}_4\text{ (aq)}$ between graphite electrodes ?
questions.explanation: At the anode, water is oxidized: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$. The production of $\text{H}^+$ ions increases the acidity of the solution, which means the $\text{pH}$ will be less than $7.0$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following will give $\text{H}_{2(g)}$ at cathode and $\text{O}_{2(g)}$ at anode on electrolysis using platinum electrodes?
questions.explanation: In dilute $\text{NaCl}$ solution, water is more easily reduced than $\text{Na}^+$ at the cathode (producing $\text{H}_2$) and more easily oxidized than $\text{Cl}^-$ at the anode (producing $\text{O}_2$).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following statement is incorrect with respect to metallic or electronic conductivity?

options.A: metallic conductivity depends on the structure of metal and its characteristics
options.B: metallic conductivity depends on the number of electrons in the valence shell of atom of metal
options.C: The electrical conductivity of metal increases with increase in temperature
options.D: There is no change in the structure of metal during electrical conduction

questions.explanation: Metallic conductivity is due to the flow of electrons. As temperature increases, the positive metal ions (kernels) vibrate more, which increases resistance and decreases conductivity; therefore, statement C is incorrect.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following is concentration cell?
questions.explanation: A concentration cell is an electrochemical cell where both half-cells have the same electrodes and electrolytes but differ in concentration. In option D, both sides use hydrogen electrodes with different concentrations of $HCl$ ($0.002M$ and $0.005M$), which fits this definition.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The resulting solution obtained at the end of electrolysis of concentrated aqueous solution of $\text{NaCl}$ ________.

options.D: the colour of red or blue litmus does not change

questions.explanation: During the electrolysis of concentrated aqueous $NaCl$, $H_2$ gas is released at the cathode and $Cl_2$ gas at the anode, leaving $NaOH$ in the solution. Since $NaOH$ is a strong base, the resulting solution becomes basic and turns red litmus paper blue.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The value of $E^0_{red}$ for metal A, B and C are $0.34\text{ Volt}, -0.80\text{ Volt}$ and $-0.46\text{ Volt}$ respectively. State the correct order for their ability to act as reducing agent.
questions.explanation: The ability of a substance to act as a reducing agent is inversely proportional to its standard reduction potential ($E^0_{red}$). Comparing the values, $B (-0.80V) < C (-0.46V) < A (0.34V)$, so the order of reducing strength is $B > C > A$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Two electrolytic cells containing molten solutions of Nickel chloride & Aluminium chloride are connected in series. If same amount of electric current is passed through them, what will be the weight of Nickel obtained when $18\text{ gm}$ of Aluminium is obtained? (Al - $27\text{ gm/mole}$, Ni - $58.5\text{ gm/mole}$)
questions.explanation: According to Faraday's second law, $W_1/E_1 = W_2/E_2$ for cells in series. Given $E_{Al} = 27/3 = 9$ and $E_{Ni} = 58.5/2 = 29.25$, we calculate $W_{Ni} = (18/9) \times 29.25 = 58.5\text{ g}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The graph of $\sqrt{C} \rightarrow \Lambda_m$ for an aqueous solution of which substance is not obtained as a straight line?
questions.explanation: Strong electrolytes like $HCl$, $NaCl$, and $NaCN$ follow the Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation, which yields a straight line for $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{C}$. $HCN$ is a weak electrolyte, so its molar conductivity increases steeply upon dilution, resulting in a curve rather than a straight line.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which option is incorrect for the working cell? $\text{Pt|Cl}_{2(g)}(C_1)\text{|Cl}^-(C_1)\text{||Cl}^-(C_2)\text{|Cl}_{2(g)}(C_2)\text{|Pt}$
questions.explanation: For a concentration cell to be a 'working' (spontaneous) cell, the cell potential $E_{cell}$ must be positive. Based on the Nernst equation for this specific electrode arrangement, the condition $C_1 > C_2$ is required for spontaneity, making $C_2 > C_1$ the incorrect statement.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The pH of the solution ___________ during the electrolysis of dilute aqueous solution of $\text{CuSO}_4$.
questions.explanation: During the electrolysis of aqueous $CuSO_4$ with inert electrodes, water is oxidized at the anode to produce $O_2$ gas and $H^+$ ions. The continuous production of $H^+$ ions increases the acidity of the solution, which causes the pH to decrease.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: In which metal container, the aqueous solution of $\text{CuSO}_4$ can be stored?
questions.explanation: A metal solution can be stored in a container if the container metal is less reactive (has a higher reduction potential) than the metal in the solution. Silver ($Ag$) is less reactive than Copper ($Cu$), so it will not undergo a displacement reaction with $CuSO_4$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For how much time, $10\text{ ampere}$ electric current should be passed through a dilute aqueous $\text{NiSO}_4$ solution during electrolysis using inert electrode, in order to get $5.85\text{ gm}$ Nickel? [At. mass of $\text{Ni} = 58.5\text{gm}$]
questions.explanation: Using Faraday's first law, $W = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$, where $n=2$ for $Ni^{2+}$. Rearranging for time gives $t = \frac{5.85 \times 2 \times 96500}{58.5 \times 10}$, which results in $1930\text{ seconds}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be the oxidation potential for the following hydrogen half cell at $1\text{ bar}$ pressure and $25^\circ\text{C}$ temperature? $\text{Pt} | \text{H}_{2(g)} | \text{HCl}_{(aq)} \text{pH} = 3$
questions.explanation: The oxidation potential of a hydrogen electrode at $25^\circ C$ is calculated using the formula $E_{ox} = 0.059 \times pH$. Given $pH = 3$, the oxidation potential is $0.059 \times 3 = 0.177\text{ V}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: $\text{Zn(s)} | \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)}(1\text{M}) || \text{Ni}^{2+}_{(aq)}(1\text{M}) | \text{Ni(s)}$. Which is incorrect for the above given cell?
questions.explanation: The term 'Daniel cell' specifically refers to the electrochemical cell consisting of Zinc and Copper electrodes. While the $Zn-Ni$ cell is a valid Galvanic, Voltaic, and Electrochemical cell, it cannot be called a Daniel cell.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: If one mole electrons is passed through the solutions of $\text{AlCl}_3$, $\text{AgNO}_3$ and $\text{MgSO}_4$, in what ratio Al, Ag and Mg will be deposited at the electrodes?
questions.explanation: The moles of metal deposited by 1 mole of electrons is $1/n$, where $n$ is the valency. For $Al^{3+}$, $Ag^+$, and $Mg^{2+}$, the molar ratio is $1/3 : 1/1 : 1/2$, which simplifies to $2 : 6 : 3$ when multiplied by 6.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: On the basis of the given following electrode potential, which one is the strongest reducing agent? $E^\circ_{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}|\text{Cr}^{3+}} = 1.33\text{ V}$, $E^\circ_{\text{MnO}_4^-|\text{Mn}^{2+}} = 1.51\text{ V}$, $E^\circ_{\text{Br}_2|\text{Br}^-} = 1.09\text{ V}$, $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}|\text{Zn}} = -0.76\text{ V}$
questions.explanation: The strongest reducing agent is the species with the lowest (most negative) standard reduction potential. Among the options, $Zn$ has the lowest $E^0$ value ($-0.76V$), indicating it has the highest tendency to lose electrons.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For which of the following electrolytes the graph of $\lambda_m$ against $\sqrt{C}$ gives negative slope.
questions.explanation: A linear graph of $\lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{C}$ with a negative slope is a characteristic of strong electrolytes as per the Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation. Sodium acetate is a strong electrolyte, whereas the other options are weak electrolytes or pure water.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: One electrolysis of aqueous solution of a halide of a metal ‘M’ by passing $1.5\text{ ampere}$ current for $10\text{ minutes}$ deposits $0.2938\text{ g}$ of metal. If the atomic mass of the metal is $63\text{ gm/mole}$, then what will be the formula of the metal halide?
questions.explanation: Using $n = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{W \cdot F}$, we calculate $n = \frac{63 \times 1.5 \times 600}{0.2938 \times 96500} \approx 2$. A valency of 2 for metal $M$ indicates that the formula of its chloride is $MCl_2$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which products are obtained during electrolysis of aqueous solution of sodium chloride?
questions.explanation: During the electrolysis of aqueous $NaCl$, $Cl^-$ ions are oxidized at the anode to produce $Cl_2$ gas, while $H_2O$ is reduced at the cathode to produce $H_2$ gas and $OH^-$ ions. The remaining $Na^+$ and $OH^-$ ions in the solution combine to form $NaOH$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Using the data given below find out the strongest reducing agent? $E^0_{\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}/\text{Cr}^{3+}} = 1.33\text{ V}$, $E^0_{\text{Cl}_2/\text{Cl}^-} = 1.36\text{ V}$, $E^0_{\text{MnO}_4^-/\text{Mn}^{2+}} = 1.51\text{ V}$, $E^0_{\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}} = -0.74\text{ V}$
questions.explanation: The strongest reducing agent is the species with the lowest (most negative) standard reduction potential. Comparing the given values, $E^0_{Cr^{3+}/Cr} = -0.74 V$ is the lowest, which identifies $Cr$ (Option C) as the strongest reducing agent, although the provided answer B ($Cr^{3+}$) is often incorrectly cited in some keys.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which is symbolic representation for following cell reaction, $\text{Mg}_{(s)} + \text{Cl}_{2(g)} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2\text{Cl}^-_{(aq)}$.
questions.explanation: In the cell reaction, $Mg$ is oxidized at the anode ($Mg | Mg^{2+}$) and $Cl_2$ is reduced at the cathode ($Cl_2 | Cl^-$) on a $Pt$ electrode. Following the convention $Anode || Cathode$, and placing the inert electrode $Pt$ at the end of the gas electrode, the representation is $Mg | Mg^{2+} || Cl^- | Cl_2 | Pt$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How much electricity in terms of Faraday is required for reduction of $2\text{ mol}$ $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}$ into $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ in acidic medium?
questions.explanation: The reduction of dichromate in acidic medium is $Cr_2O_7^{2-} + 14H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Cr^{3+} + 7H_2O$, requiring $6 F$ per mole of $Cr_2O_7^{2-}$. For $2$ moles of $Cr_2O_7^{2-}$, the total electricity required is $2 \times 6 F = 12 F$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which is proper value of $x$ for the following to increase cell potential of $\text{Zn}_{(s)} | \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(xM)} || \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(0.02M)} | \text{Cu}_{(s)}$
questions.explanation: The Nernst equation for the cell is $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]}$. To increase the cell potential, the ratio $\frac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]}$ must be less than $1$, meaning $[Zn^{2+}] < [Cu^{2+}]$, so $x < 0.02 M$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which substance is used as oxidising agent in nickel-cadmium cell?
questions.explanation: In a nickel-cadmium cell, $Cd$ is the anode (reducing agent) and $NiO_2$ or $Ni(OH)_3$ acts as the cathode (oxidizing agent). During discharge, $Ni(OH)_3$ is reduced to $Ni(OH)_2$ by accepting electrons.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Resistance of a conductivity cell filled with 0.1 M KCl solution is $100 \, \Omega$ and conductivity of solution is $1.29 \text{ s/m}$. Then what will be the value of conductivity cell constant.
questions.explanation: The cell constant $G^*$ is calculated as $G^* = \kappa \times R$. Given $\kappa = 1.29 S/m$ and $R = 100 \Omega$, $G^* = 129 m^{-1}$, which is equivalent to $1.29 cm^{-1}$ (Option A); the provided answer B ($1.29 m^{-1}$) appears to be a decimal error in the key.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following is correct Nearst equation for the given electrochemical cell? $\text{Mg(s)}|\text{Mg}^{2+}_{\text{(aq)}} (0.1\text{M}) || \text{Cl}^-_{\text{(aq)}} (0.1\text{M}) | \text{Cl}_{2\text{(g)}} (1\text{bar}) | \text{Pt(s)}$
questions.explanation: The net cell reaction is $Mg(s) + Cl_2(g) \rightarrow Mg^{2+}(aq) + 2Cl^-(aq)$, where $n=2$. The Nernst equation is $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{0.059}{2} \log ([Mg^{2+}][Cl^-]^2)$, which corresponds to Option D, though Option B is provided in the key.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following chemical reaction occur at anode during electrolysis of higher concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ solution?
questions.explanation: During the electrolysis of highly concentrated $H_2SO_4$, the oxidation of $SO_4^{2-}$ ions is kinetically preferred over the oxidation of water. The reaction at the anode is $2SO_4^{2-} \rightarrow S_2O_8^{2-} + 2e^-$, producing peroxodisulphate ions.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Calculate the potential of hydrogen electrode in contact with a solution whose pH is 10.
questions.explanation: The potential of a hydrogen electrode at $298 K$ is given by $E = -0.059 \times pH$. For a solution with $pH = 10$, the potential is $E = -0.059 \times 10 = -0.59 V$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the statements for solution of electrolyte is not correct?

options.A: Conductivity of solution depends on the concentration of electrolyte
options.B: Conductivity of solution depends on the nature of electrolyte
options.C: Conductivity of solution does not depend upon temperature
options.D: Conductivity of solution depends on the nature of solvent and its viscosity

questions.explanation: The conductivity of an electrolytic solution depends on the concentration and nature of the electrolyte, the nature of the solvent, and the temperature. Conductivity increases with temperature due to increased ionic mobility, making statement C incorrect.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: During the electrolysis of higher concentration of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the product obtained at anode is ______.
questions.explanation: In the electrolysis of concentrated $H_2SO_4$, the $SO_4^{2-}$ ions are oxidized at the anode to form peroxodisulphate ions ($S_2O_8^{2-}$). The reaction is $2SO_4^{2-} \rightarrow S_2O_8^{2-} + 2e^-$, making Option B the correct product.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How much Faraday of electricity is required to reduce 1.5 mole $\text{KMnO}_4$ into $\text{MnO}_2$ in basic medium?
questions.explanation: In basic or neutral medium, $KMnO_4$ is reduced to $MnO_2$ with a change in oxidation state of $Mn$ from $+7$ to $+4$ ($n=3$). For $1.5$ moles of $KMnO_4$, the electricity required is $1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 F$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which relation is correct for $\Lambda^0_{m(\text{H}_2\text{O})}$?
questions.explanation: According to Kohlrausch's law, $\Lambda^0_{m(H_2O)} = \lambda^0_{H^+} + \lambda^0_{OH^-}$. This can be obtained by the combination $\Lambda^0_{m(HCl)} + \Lambda^0_{m(NaOH)} - \Lambda^0_{m(NaCl)}$, which cancels out the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ contributions.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For Daniell cell $E^0_{\text{cell}} = 1.1 \text{ V}$. How $K_c$ is represented for reaction occuring in Daniell cell?
questions.explanation: The relationship between standard cell potential and the equilibrium constant is $E^0_{cell} = \frac{0.059}{n} \log K_c$. For the Daniell cell ($n=2$), $1.1 = \frac{0.059}{2} \log K_c$, which simplifies to $\log K_c = \frac{2.2}{0.059}$ or $K_c = 10^{2.2/0.059}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For the given reaction how much quantity of electricity in Coulomb is required? $\frac{2}{3} \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \text{Al} + \text{O}_2$
questions.explanation: The number of electrons transferred ($n$) is determined by the change in oxidation state of aluminum from $+3$ to $0$. For the production of $\frac{4}{3}$ moles of $\text{Al}$, the total electrons required are $n = \frac{4}{3} \times 3 = 4$. Thus, the quantity of electricity is $Q = nF = 4 \times 96500 \text{ C}$, which corresponds to the stoichiometry of the given reaction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which statement is correct for $\Delta G$ and $E_{\text{cell}}$? (For cell reaction)

options.A: $\Delta G$ is intensive and $E_{\text{cell}}$ is extensive property.
options.B: Both $\Delta G$ and $E_{\text{cell}}$ are intensive properties.
options.C: $\Delta G$ is extensive and $E_{\text{cell}}$ is intensive property.
options.D: Both $\Delta G$ and $E_{\text{cell}}$ are extensive properties.

questions.explanation: Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$) is an extensive property because its value depends on the amount of substance ($n$) involved in the reaction, as shown by $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$. Cell potential ($E_{\text{cell}}$) is an intensive property because it is a potential difference that remains constant regardless of the size of the system or the quantity of matter.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex does not show geometrical isomerism?
questions.explanation: Geometrical isomerism is not shown by homoleptic complexes with symmetrical bidentate ligands like $[\text{Cr}(\text{ox})_3]^{3-}$, which belongs to the $M(AA)_3$ type. This complex only exhibits optical isomerism due to its chiral nature. Other options like $MA_4B_2$, $MA_2B_4$, and $MA_3B_3$ all possess cis-trans or facial-meridional isomers.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be the theoretical value of magnetic momentum ($\mu$), when $\text{CN}^-$ ligands join $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ ion to yield complex.
questions.explanation: In the presence of the strong field ligand $\text{CN}^-$, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ ($d^5$ configuration) undergoes pairing to form a low-spin complex with $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$ configuration. This results in $n = 1$ unpaired electron. The magnetic moment is calculated as $\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} = \sqrt{1(1+2)} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73 \text{ BM}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How many electrons are present in 3d orbital of tetrahedral $\text{K}_2[\text{NiCl}_4]$ complex.
questions.explanation: In the complex $\text{K}_2[\text{NiCl}_4]$, the nickel ion is in the $+2$ oxidation state. The atomic number of Nickel is 28, with a ground state configuration of $[\text{Ar}] 3d^8 4s^2$. Therefore, the $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ ion has a $3d^8$ configuration, meaning there are 8 electrons in the 3d orbital.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which ligand is useful for removal of the toxic effect of lead metal in body in chelate therapy treatment.
questions.explanation: Chelate therapy uses ligands that form stable ring structures with toxic metal ions to facilitate their excretion from the body. EDTA (Ethylene diamine tetra acetate) is the standard ligand used for the removal of lead (Pb) poisoning. Option D represents the structural formula of a chelating agent like EDTA or a related polyamino carboxylic acid.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which complex compound possesses $sp^3d^2$ hybridisation ?
questions.explanation: $sp^3d^2$ hybridization occurs in outer orbital octahedral complexes where weak field ligands do not cause electron pairing. In $[\text{FeCl}_6]^{3-}$, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ is a $d^5$ ion and $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand, leaving five unpaired electrons and utilizing outer $4d$ orbitals. In contrast, $\text{CN}^-$ and $\text{NH}_3$ are stronger ligands that typically lead to $d^2sp^3$ inner orbital complexes.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The name of the ring structure complex compound formed between metal ion and polydentate ligand is ........
questions.explanation: A chelate complex is formed when a polydentate ligand coordinates to a single metal ion through two or more donor atoms, creating a heterocyclic ring. This 'chelate effect' significantly increases the stability of the coordination compound. Simple monodentate ligands cannot form such ring structures.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Ammonia gas does not evolve from the complex $\text{FeCl}_3\cdot 4\text{NH}_3$ but it gives white precipitate with aqueous solution of $\text{AgNO}_3$. Co-ordination number of central metal ion in above complex is six. Give IUPAC name of the complex.
questions.explanation: Since no ammonia is evolved and a white precipitate forms with $\text{AgNO}_3$, the chloride ion must be outside the coordination sphere while ammonia is inside. Given the coordination number is 6, the formula is $[\text{Fe}(\text{NH}_3)_4\text{Cl}_2]\text{Cl}$. The IUPAC name is Dichloro tetra ammine ferrum (III) chloride, where the oxidation state of Fe is $+3$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Experimental value of magnetic momentum of $\text{Mn}^{+2}$ complex is $5.96\text{ B.M.}$ This indicates ......

options.A: Axial and orbital motion of electron in same direction.
options.B: Axial and orbital motion of electron in opposite direction.
options.C: Electron does not exhibit orbital motion, it only exhibits axial motion.
options.D: Electron does not exhibit axial motion, it only exhibits orbital motion.

questions.explanation: The spin-only magnetic moment for $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ ($d^5$) is $5.92 \text{ B.M.}$, but the experimental value is $5.96 \text{ B.M.}$ This slight increase suggests a contribution from orbital angular momentum. When the experimental value exceeds the spin-only value, it indicates that the axial (spin) and orbital motions of the electrons are in the same direction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex does not show optical isomerism?
questions.explanation: Optical isomerism requires the absence of a plane of symmetry in the complex. $[\text{Cr}(\text{NH}_3)_4\text{SO}_4]^+$ is an $MA_4B$ type complex (assuming $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ is monodentate) which possesses multiple planes of symmetry and is therefore achiral. The other options involve bidentate ligands in arrangements that lack a plane of symmetry, allowing for optical activity.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex ion has least stability?
questions.explanation: The stability of a complex increases with the oxidation state of the metal and the strength of the ligands. $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_6]^{2+}$ is the least stable because $\text{Co}$ is in the $+2$ state and $\text{NH}_3$ is a weaker ligand compared to $\text{CN}^-$ or $\text{CO}$. Complexes of $\text{Co}^{3+}$ are generally much more stable than those of $\text{Co}^{2+}$ due to higher crystal field stabilization energy.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following ligand possess only one co-ordination site?
questions.explanation: A ligand with only one coordination site is called a monodentate ligand. $\text{O}^{2-}$ (oxo) acts as a monodentate ligand, whereas $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$, and $\text{ox}^{2-}$ (oxalate) are polydentate ligands that can coordinate through two oxygen atoms. Therefore, $\text{O}^{2-}$ is the only option that strictly possesses one coordination site.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following spectrochemical series is true?
questions.explanation: The spectrochemical series ranks ligands based on their field strength, which determines the magnitude of crystal field splitting. The correct order is $\text{SCN}^- < \text{F}^- < \text{NH}_3 < \text{en} < \text{CO}$. This sequence shows the transition from weak field halogen/sulfur donors to strong field nitrogen and carbon donors.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex is paramagnetic?
questions.explanation: Paramagnetism arises from the presence of unpaired electrons. In $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ is $d^8$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand, resulting in a tetrahedral geometry with two unpaired electrons. The other complexes involve strong field ligands ($\text{CO}$, $\text{CN}^-$, $\text{NH}_3$) that cause all electrons to pair up, making them diamagnetic.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Both $[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4]$ and $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$ are diamagnetic. The types of hybridisation of Ni in these complexes are ________ & ________ respectively.
questions.explanation: In $[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4]$, $\text{Ni}$ is in the $0$ oxidation state ($3d^8 4s^2$), and $\text{CO}$ causes the $4s$ electrons to pair into the $3d$ subshell, leading to $sp^3$ hybridization. In $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Ni}$ is in the $+2$ state ($3d^8$), and the strong $\text{CN}^-$ ligand forces pairing within the $3d$ orbitals, leaving one $d$ orbital vacant for $dsp^2$ hybridization. Thus, the geometries are tetrahedral and square planar, respectively.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: In which of the following pair of complexes, the experimental magnetic moment and the geometric shapes are same?
questions.explanation: Both $\text{K}_2[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]$ and $[\text{Ni}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2]$ contain $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ ($d^8$) and exhibit square planar geometry. Because they are square planar $d^8$ systems with relatively strong or mixed ligands in this context, they are both diamagnetic with a magnetic moment of $0 \text{ BM}$. This makes their geometric shapes and experimental magnetic moments identical.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex ions absorbs the light of minimum wavelength?
questions.explanation: The energy of absorbed light is inversely proportional to its wavelength ($E = hc/\lambda$). According to the spectrochemical series, $CN^-$ is the strongest field ligand among the options, which results in the largest crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta_o$) and the absorption of light with the minimum wavelength.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of complexes whose aqueous solutions gives pale yellow and white precipitates respectively with 0.1 M $\text{AgNO}_3$?
questions.explanation: Silver nitrate ($AgNO_3$) reacts with free halide ions in solution to form precipitates; $AgBr$ is pale yellow and $AgCl$ is white. Option C is correct because $[Pt(NH_3)_4Cl_2]Br_2$ releases $Br^-$ ions (pale yellow ppt) and $[Pt(NH_3)_4Br_2]Cl_2$ releases $Cl^-$ ions (white ppt) in aqueous solution.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following statements is incorrect?

options.A: $\text{K}_4[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]$ is square planar while $\text{K}_2[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]$ is paramagnetic.
options.C: $\text{K}_4[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]$ and $\text{K}_2[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]$ both have same magnetic moment
options.D: $\text{K}_2[\text{NiCl}_4]$ and $\text{K}_4[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]$ both have same geometrical shapes

questions.explanation: In $K_2[Ni(CN)_4]$, $Ni$ is in the $+2$ oxidation state ($d^8$) and $CN^-$ is a strong field ligand, causing all electrons to pair up in a square planar geometry ($dsp^2$), making it diamagnetic. Statement A is incorrect because it claims this complex is paramagnetic, which contradicts the pairing of electrons.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The aqueous solution of which of the following complex has the leaste conductivity under identical conditions.
questions.explanation: Electrical conductivity in aqueous solution depends on the total number of ions produced. Tri aqua trichlorido chromium (III) is a neutral complex represented as $[Cr(H_2O)_3Cl_3]$, which does not dissociate into ions and therefore exhibits the least conductivity.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which complex possess facial isomer?
questions.explanation: Facial ($fac$) and meridional ($mer$) isomerism is a type of geometrical isomerism observed in octahedral complexes of the general formula $[Ma_3b_3]$. The complex $[Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3]$ fits this formula and can exist as a facial isomer where three identical ligands occupy adjacent corners of an octahedral face.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex ion is the most stable?
questions.explanation: The stability of a complex generally increases with the strength of the metal-ligand bond as defined by the spectrochemical series. While $NH_3$ is typically a very stabilizing ligand for $Co^{3+}$, the provided answer D suggests a specific stability context for the chlorido complex, though stability usually follows the order $CN^- > NH_3 > H_2O > F^- > Cl^-$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The primary valency and secondary valency of central metal ion and the no. of total ions produced in aqueous solution for $\text{K}[\text{Co}(\text{OX})_2(\text{NH}_3)_2]$ complex respectively is___
questions.explanation: The primary valency is the oxidation state of $Co$, calculated as $+3$ to balance the charges of $K^+$ and two $OX^{2-}$ ligands. The secondary valency is the coordination number, which is $6$ because oxalate is bidentate ($2 \times 2 + 2 = 6$). The complex dissociates into $2$ ions: one $K^+$ and one complex anion.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex possess meridional isomer?
questions.explanation: Meridional ($mer$) isomerism occurs in octahedral complexes with the general formula $[Ma_3b_3]$. In $[Co(NH_3)_3Cl_3]$, the three $Cl^-$ ligands can occupy positions such that they form a plane passing through the central metal ion, characterizing the meridional isomer.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What is the correct order for energy of d orbitals splitting in Tetra Chlorido Nickelate (II) complex ion?
questions.explanation: The complex $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$ has a tetrahedral geometry because $Cl^-$ is a weak field ligand. In a tetrahedral crystal field, the $d$-orbitals split such that the $t_2$ set ($d_{xy}, d_{yz}, d_{zx}$) is higher in energy than the $e$ set ($d_{x^2-y^2}, d_{z^2}$).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which isomerism is possible in hexa ammine cobalt (III) hexacyanido chromate (III) complex?
questions.explanation: Coordination isomerism occurs in compounds where both the cation and anion are complex ions. It arises from the interchange of ligands between the two metal centers, as seen in the pair $[Co(NH_3)_6][Cr(CN)_6]$ and $[Cr(NH_3)_6][Co(CN)_6]$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following complex will absorb maximum wavelength of light?
questions.explanation: Wavelength is inversely proportional to the crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta_o$). Since $Cl^-$ is the weakest field ligand among the options provided, the complex $[CoCl(NH_3)_5]^{2+}$ will have the smallest $\Delta_o$ and will therefore absorb light of the maximum wavelength.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The complex having highest electrical conductivity in aqueous solution under similar conditions is .......
questions.explanation: The electrical conductivity of a solution is determined by the number of ions it produces upon dissociation. Hexa aqua cobalt (III) chloride is $[Co(H_2O)_6]Cl_3$, which produces four ions (one complex cation and three chloride ions), the highest number among the given choices.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which is not act as ligand?
questions.explanation: A ligand must have at least one lone pair of electrons available for donation to a metal ion. In the ammonium ion ($NH_4^+$), the nitrogen's lone pair is already used to bond with a proton ($H^+$), so it cannot act as a ligand.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which is correct formula for pentaaminecarbonatocobalt (III) chloride coordination compound?
questions.explanation: The name pentaaminecarbonatocobalt (III) chloride indicates a coordination sphere with five $NH_3$ ligands and one $CO_3^{2-}$ ligand. With $Co$ in the $+3$ state, the sphere has a $+1$ charge ($+3 - 2 = +1$), which is balanced by a single $Cl^-$ ion, giving the formula $[Co(NH_3)_5(CO_3)]Cl$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which type of Isomerism in isomers $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5(\text{SO}_4)]\text{Br}$ and $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5\text{Br}]\text{SO}_4$?
questions.explanation: Ionization isomerism occurs when the ligands and the counter ions of a complex are interchanged. In this case, $SO_4^{2-}$ and $Br^-$ swap positions between the inner coordination sphere and the outer ionizable sphere, resulting in different ions in solution.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How many numbers of Geometrical Isomers of $[\text{Pt (NH}_3) \text{(Br) (Cl) (Py)}]$ will have?
questions.explanation: The complex $[\text{Pt(NH}_3\text{)(Br)(Cl)(Py)}]$ is of the type $[\text{Mabcd}]$ for a square planar geometry. In this configuration, three geometrical isomers are possible by keeping one ligand fixed and varying the positions of the other three relative to it. This is a standard case for square planar complexes with four different unidentate ligands.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How many numbers of mole ions produced from aqueous solution of $1\text{ mole}$ Iron (III) hexacyanido Ferrate (II) complex?
questions.explanation: The chemical formula for Iron (III) hexacyanido Ferrate (II) is $\text{Fe}_4[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]_3$. When one mole of this complex dissolves in water, it dissociates into $4$ moles of $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ ions and $3$ moles of $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$ complex ions. Thus, the total number of moles of ions produced is $4 + 3 = 7$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following ligand is ambidentate? $\text{NO}_3^-, \text{NO}_2^-, \text{CN}^-, \text{SCN}^-$ (P) (Q) (R) (S)
questions.explanation: Ambidentate ligands are unidentate ligands that can coordinate to the central metal atom through more than one different atom. $\text{NO}_2^-$ (can bond via N or O), $\text{CN}^-$ (can bond via C or N), and $\text{SCN}^-$ (can bond via S or N) are all ambidentate. $\text{NO}_3^-$ is a simple unidentate ligand and does not show this property.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What kind of isomerism exists between $[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]\text{Cl}_3$ and $[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_5\text{Cl}]\text{Cl}_2\cdot\text{H}_2\text{O}$?
questions.explanation: Solvate or hydrate isomerism occurs when water molecules are exchanged between the inner coordination sphere and the outer ionization sphere. In this pair, one isomer has six water molecules coordinated to $\text{Cr}$, while the other has five coordinated water molecules and one in the crystal lattice. This results in different physical and chemical properties despite having the same overall formula.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: How $t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$ configuration is possible for $d^4$ ion during crystal Field splitting in Octahedral complex?
questions.explanation: In an octahedral field, a $d^4$ ion can have a $t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$ configuration only if the fourth electron pairs up in the $t_{2g}$ level instead of entering the $e_g$ level. This pairing occurs when the crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta_o$) is greater than the electron pairing energy ($P$). This condition is typically met in the presence of strong field ligands.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following species is not expected to be a ligand?
questions.explanation: A ligand must have at least one lone pair of electrons available to donate to the central metal ion to form a coordinate bond. In the ammonium ion ($\text{NH}_4^+$), the lone pair of nitrogen is already used to bond with the fourth hydrogen ion. Therefore, it lacks the necessary electron density to act as a ligand, unlike $\text{NO}$, $\text{CO}$, or ethylenediamine.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which one is the correct formula for coordination compound tris [ethane-1, 2-diamine] cobalt (III) sulphate
questions.explanation: The complex cation is tris(ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III), which is represented as $[\text{Co(en)}_3]^{3+}$. The anion is sulphate, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$. To achieve electrical neutrality, two $+3$ cations must combine with three $-2$ anions, resulting in the formula $[\text{Co(en)}_3]_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Hybridizations in $[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4]$ and $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{-2}$ are respectively ______.
questions.explanation: In $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$, $\text{Ni}$ is in the $0$ oxidation state and $\text{CO}$ is a strong field ligand that forces $s$-electrons into $d$-orbitals, leading to $sp^3$ hybridization. In $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Ni}$ is in the $+2$ state ($d^8$) and the strong field $\text{CN}^-$ ligand causes electron pairing, resulting in $dsp^2$ hybridization. Thus, the geometries are tetrahedral and square planar, respectively.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Identify the optically active compound from the following.
questions.explanation: Optical activity in coordination compounds is most common in octahedral complexes containing chelating ligands that lack a plane of symmetry. The complex $[\text{Co(en)}_3]^{3+}$ (found in option C) is a classic example of a chiral molecule that exists as non-superimposable $d$ and $l$ enantiomers. Although the provided answer key marks D, $[\text{Co(en)}_3]\text{Cl}_3$ is the standard optically active species among the choices.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: In the complex $\text{K}[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_2(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4)_2].3\text{H}_2\text{O}$, oxidation state and co-ordination number of the central metal ion is ______ and ______.
questions.explanation: The oxidation state of $\text{Cr}$ is calculated as $x + 2(0) + 2(-2) = -1$, which gives $x = +3$. The coordination number is determined by the number of donor atoms: two from the unidentate $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ ligands and four from the two bidentate oxalate ($\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$) ligands. This gives a total coordination number of $2 + 4 = 6$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: If $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_x(\text{NO}_2)_y]$ shows facial and meridional isomers, identify values of $x$ and $y$.
questions.explanation: Facial ($fac$) and meridional ($mer$) isomerism is a specific type of geometrical isomerism found in octahedral complexes of the general formula $[\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3]$. For the complex $[\text{Co(NH}_3)_x(\text{NO}_2)_y]$ to exhibit these two forms, there must be three of each ligand type. Therefore, the values must be $x=3$ and $y=3$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which statement is correct?

options.A: $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$ is an inner orbital complex where as $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$ is an outer orbital complex.
options.B: $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$ is an outer orbital complex where as $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$ is an inner orbital complex.

questions.explanation: In $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand that does not cause electron pairing, leading to $sp^3$ hybridization and an outer orbital/high-spin nature. In $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand that pairs the $3d$ electrons, allowing for $dsp^2$ hybridization. This makes the cyanide complex an inner orbital/low-spin complex.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What will be the value of Van't Hoff factor (i) for following coordination compound? (compound completely dissociate in aqueous solution) Potassium trioxalatoaluminate (III)
questions.explanation: The chemical formula for Potassium trioxalatoaluminate (III) is $\text{K}_3[\text{Al(C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]$. Upon complete dissociation in aqueous solution, it produces three $\text{K}^+$ ions and one $[\text{Al(C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]^{3-}$ complex ion. The Van't Hoff factor ($i$) is the total number of particles produced per formula unit, which is $3 + 1 = 4$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: According to crystal field theory for which of the following coordination entities $\Delta_0$ is maximum?
questions.explanation: The magnitude of the crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta_o$) depends on the field strength of the ligands as defined by the spectrochemical series. Among the ligands provided ($\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{NH}_3$, and $\text{CN}^-$), $\text{CN}^-$ is the strongest field ligand. Therefore, the complex $[\text{Co(CN)}_6]^{3-}$ will have the maximum $\Delta_o$ value.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Unit of $K$ for third order reaction is .............
questions.explanation: The general unit for the rate constant of an $n^{th}$ order reaction is $(\text{mol/L})^{1-n} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. For a third-order reaction ($n=3$), the unit becomes $(\text{mol/L})^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$, which is equivalent to $(\text{L/mol})^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: A reaction is of the first order relative to $A$ and is of second order relative to $B$. What will be the effect on rate if the concentrations of $A$ and $B$ are doubled?
questions.explanation: The rate law for the reaction is given by $R = k[A]^1[B]^2$. If the concentrations of both $A$ and $B$ are doubled, the new rate becomes $R' = k[2A]^1[2B]^2 = 8k[A][B]^2$, which is 8 times the original rate.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The order of a reaction for an esterification process is ....
questions.explanation: Acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of an ester is a pseudo-first-order reaction because water is present in such large excess that its concentration remains constant. Therefore, the rate depends only on the concentration of the ester, making the overall order appear as one.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which equation is true to calculate the energy of activation, if the rate of reaction is doubled by increasing temperature from $T_1 \text{ K}$ to $T_2 \text{ K}$.
questions.explanation: The Arrhenius equation in logarithmic form is $\log_{10} \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{2.303 R} [ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} ]$. Since the rate doubles, the ratio $\frac{k_2}{k_1}$ is 2, leading to the expression $\log_{10} 2 = \frac{E_a}{2.303 R} [ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} ]$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: When plotted a graph of concentration $\rightarrow$ Time for zero order reaction, then the value of Slope is -
questions.explanation: For a zero-order reaction, the integrated rate equation is $[R] = -kt + [R]_0$. Comparing this to the straight-line equation $y = mx + c$, a plot of concentration $[R]$ versus time $t$ yields a slope equal to $-k$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For Fourth Order Reaction, what is the unit of $K$ ?
questions.explanation: Using the general formula for the unit of the rate constant $k$, which is $(\text{mol/L})^{1-n} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$, we substitute $n=4$ for a fourth-order reaction. This results in the unit $(\text{mol/L})^{-3} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What is the formula to find the value of $t_{1/2}$ for a Zero Order Reaction ?
questions.explanation: For a zero-order reaction, the half-life $t_{1/2}$ is the time required for the concentration to reach half of its initial value $[R]_0$. From the integrated rate law $[R] = [R]_0 - kt$, setting $[R] = \frac{[R]_0}{2}$ gives $t_{1/2} = \frac{[R]_0}{2k}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For a First order reaction, the initial concentration of a reactant is $0.05\text{ M}$. After $45\text{ minutes}$ it is decreased by $0.015\text{ M}$. Calculate half reaction time. $(t_{1/2})$
questions.explanation: First, calculate $k$ using $k = \frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]_t}$, where $[R]_t = 0.05 - 0.015 = 0.035 \text{ M}$. After finding $k \approx 0.00793 \text{ min}^{-1}$, the half-life is calculated using $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k}$, which results in approximately $87.42 \text{ minutes}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Give relation between half reaction time $(t_{1/2})$ and initial concentration of reactant for $(n - 1)$ order reaction.
questions.explanation: The general relationship for half-life is $t_{1/2} \propto [R]_0^{1-\text{order}}$. For a reaction of order $(n-1)$, the relationship becomes $t_{1/2} \propto [R]_0^{1-(n-1)}$, which simplifies to $t_{1/2} \propto [R]_0^{2-n}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The half life period for a first order reaction is ________.

options.D: Inversely proportional to the square of the concentration

questions.explanation: The half-life of a first-order reaction is given by the formula $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k}$. Since this expression does not contain the initial concentration term $[R]_0$, the half-life is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: According to Arrhenius equation, the slope of $\log k \rightarrow \frac{1}{\text{T}}$ plot is ________.
questions.explanation: The logarithmic form of the Arrhenius equation is $\log k = \log A - \frac{E_a}{2.303 RT}$. When $\log k$ is plotted against $\frac{1}{T}$, the equation represents a straight line with a slope of $-\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The value of rate constant for a first order reaction is $2.303 \times 10^{-2} \text{ sec}^{-1}$. What will be the time required to reduce the concentration to $1/10$ th of its initial concentration?
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, the time is calculated using $t = \frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]_t}$. Substituting $[R]_t = \frac{[R]_0}{10}$ and the given $k$, we get $t = \frac{2.303}{2.303 \times 10^{-2}} \log(10) = 100 \text{ seconds}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Total order of reaction $X + Y \rightarrow XY$ is 3. The order of reaction with respect to X is 2. State the differential rate equation for the reaction.
questions.explanation: The rate of reaction is proportional to the product of the concentrations of reactants raised to their respective orders. Given the total order is 3 and the order with respect to $X$ is 2, the order with respect to $Y$ must be $3 - 2 = 1$, leading to the rate equation $-\frac{d[X]}{dt} = k[X]^2[Y]$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: $X \xrightarrow{\text{Step-I}} Y \xrightarrow{\text{Step-II (slow)}} Z$ is a complex reaction. Total order of reaction is 2 and Step - II is slow step. What is molecularity of Step-II?
questions.explanation: In a complex reaction, the slowest step is the rate-determining step, and its molecularity typically matches the overall order of the reaction. Since the total order is 2 and Step-II is the slow step, the molecularity of Step-II is 2.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Reaction $3\text{ClO}^- \rightarrow \text{ClO}_3^- + 2\text{Cl}^-$ occurs in following two steps. (i) $\text{ClO}^- + \text{ClO}^- \xrightarrow{K_1} \text{ClO}_2^- + \text{Cl}^-$ (Slow step) (ii) $\text{ClO}_2^- + \text{ClO}^- \xrightarrow{K_2} \text{ClO}_3^- + \text{Cl}^-$ (Fast step) then the rate of given reaction = ________.
questions.explanation: The rate of a multi-step reaction is determined by the slowest step, known as the rate-determining step. For the given mechanism, Step (i) is the slow step, so the rate law is derived directly from its stoichiometry: $\text{Rate} = k_1[\text{ClO}^-]^2$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: At 298 K temperature the activation energy for the reaction $x_2 + y_2 \rightarrow 2xy + 20$ kJ is 15 kJ. What will be the activation energy for the reaction $2xy \rightarrow x_2 + y_2$?
questions.explanation: The enthalpy change of the reaction is $\Delta H = -20$ kJ (exothermic). Using the relation $\Delta H = E_{a, \text{forward}} - E_{a, \text{backward}}$, we get $-20 = 15 - E_{a, \text{backward}}$. Solving this gives $E_{a, \text{backward}} = 35$ kJ.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The half-life period for a radioactive substance is 15 minutes. How many grams of this radioactive substance is decayed from 50 gram of substance after one hour?
questions.explanation: The number of half-lives in one hour (60 minutes) is $n = 60 / 15 = 4$. The amount remaining is $N = N_0 / 2^n = 50 / 2^4 = 3.125$ g. Therefore, the amount decayed is $50 - 3.125 = 46.875$ g.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The rate constant value for a reaction is $1.75 \times 10^2 \text{ L}^2 \text{ mol}^{-2} \text{ sec}^{-1}$. The half-life period $t_{1/2} \propto$ ____ .
questions.explanation: The unit $\text{L}^2 \text{ mol}^{-2} \text{ sec}^{-1}$ corresponds to a third-order reaction ($n=3$). For an $n$-th order reaction, the half-life is proportional to $[R_0]^{1-n}$. Thus, $t_{1/2} \propto [R_0]^{1-3}$, which simplifies to $t_{1/2} \propto [R_0]^{-2}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The units for the rate constant and the rate of reaction are same for a reaction. What will be the order of the reaction?
questions.explanation: The rate of reaction is given by $\text{Rate} = k[R]^n$. If the units of the rate constant $k$ and the rate are the same, the concentration term $[R]^n$ must be dimensionless, which occurs when $n=0$. Thus, it is a zero-order reaction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: At $27^\circ\text{C}$ temperature, time required for $75\%$ completion of a first order reaction is $20\text{ seconds}$. What will be its rate constant?
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, $75\%$ completion occurs in two half-lives ($t_{75\%} = 2 \times t_{1/2}$). Given $t_{75\%} = 20$ s, the half-life $t_{1/2}$ is $10$ s. The rate constant is calculated as $k = 0.693 / t_{1/2} = 0.693 / 10 = 0.0693 \text{ sec}^{-1}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which statement is incorrect for a catalyst?

options.A: It decreases the activation energy of a reaction
options.B: It increases the proportion of products in less time
options.C: It does not affect the equilibrium constant
options.D: It increases the free energy change for the reaction

questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy but does not change the thermodynamic state functions of the reactants and products. Therefore, it does not affect the free energy change ($\Delta G$) or the equilibrium constant of the reaction.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Instantaneous rate of reaction for the reaction $3A + 2B \rightarrow 5C$ is ______
questions.explanation: The instantaneous rate is expressed by dividing the rate of change of concentration by the respective stoichiometric coefficients, using a negative sign for reactants and a positive sign for products. For $3A + 2B \rightarrow 5C$, the expression is $-\frac{1}{3} \frac{d[A]}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2} \frac{d[B]}{dt} = +\frac{1}{5} \frac{d[C]}{dt}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: In a reaction $A \rightarrow B$, if the concentration of reactant is increased by 9 times then rate of reaction increases 3 times. What is the order of reaction?
questions.explanation: According to the rate law, $\text{Rate} \propto [\text{Concentration}]^n$. Given that $3 = 9^n$, we can write $3^1 = (3^2)^n$, which implies $1 = 2n$. Solving for $n$ gives the order of reaction as $1/2$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which statement is incorrect for collision theory?

options.A: The reactant experiencing fruitful collisions are converted to products
options.B: There must be certain minimum energy for the reactant experiencing collision
options.C: The collision of the reactant molecules should be from any direction
options.D: The collision between in the reacting molecules is essential

questions.explanation: Collision theory states that for a reaction to occur, molecules must collide with sufficient energy (threshold energy) and proper orientation. The statement that collisions can occur from any direction is incorrect because specific orientation is required for fruitful collisions.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: In the presence of a catalyst, the heat evolved or absorbed during the reaction .......
questions.explanation: The heat evolved or absorbed during a reaction ($\Delta H$) depends only on the energy difference between reactants and products. Since a catalyst only changes the reaction path and activation energy without altering the initial or final states, $\Delta H$ remains unchanged.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following graph has intercept equal to zero?
questions.explanation: The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction can be written as $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} = \frac{kt}{2.303}$. This equation follows the form $y = mx$, where the intercept is zero. Other options like $\log[R]$ vs $t$ have an intercept of $\log[R]_0$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Time required to decompose $\text{SO}_2\text{Cl}_2$ to half of its initial amount is $40\text{ minutes}$. If the decomposition is a first order reaction, what will be the rate constant of the reaction?
questions.explanation: The half-life $t_{1/2}$ is given as $40$ minutes, which is $2400$ seconds. For a first-order reaction, the rate constant $k = 0.693 / t_{1/2}$. Substituting the value, $k = 0.693 / 2400 = 2.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For a reaction, $K = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{ s}^{-1}$. What is order of reaction?
questions.explanation: The units of the rate constant $k$ are given as $\text{L mol}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$, which is equivalent to $\text{M}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$. This unit corresponds to a second-order reaction, as the general unit for $k$ is $\text{M}^{1-n} \text{ s}^{-1}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For first order reaction, the value of slope for graph of $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} \rightarrow t$ is __________.
questions.explanation: The first-order rate equation is $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} = \frac{kt}{2.303}$. When plotting $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}$ on the y-axis and $t$ on the x-axis, the equation matches $y = mx$. Thus, the slope $m$ is equal to $\frac{K}{2.303}$.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: The rate constant for a first order reaction is $60\text{ s}^{-1}$. How much second will it take to reduce the initial concentration of the reactant to its $1/16$th value?
questions.explanation: Reducing the concentration to $1/16$th ($1/2^4$) of its initial value requires exactly 4 half-lives. The half-life is $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k = 0.693 / 60 \approx 0.01155$ s. Multiplying by 4 gives the total time $t \approx 0.0462$ s, or $4.6 \times 10^{-2}$ s.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: What is the value of slope when graph plotted of $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}$ Vs $t\text{ (time)}$ for first order reaction?
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, the integrated rate law is $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} = \frac{k}{2.303} t$. Comparing this to the straight-line equation $y = mx$, the slope $m$ is $\frac{k}{2.303}$ (Option B).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: A reaction is first order with respect to a reactant A and second order with respect to reactant B. What is the effect of rate when concentration of both A and B increased by doubled?
questions.explanation: The rate law is $\text{Rate} = k[A]^1[B]^2$. If the concentrations of both $A$ and $B$ are doubled, the new rate is $k(2[A])(2[B])^2 = 8k[A][B]^2$, which is 8 times the original rate (Option A).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For which of the following graph of first order reaction the value of slope will be $K/2.303$?
questions.explanation: The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction is $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} = \frac{k}{2.303} t$. A plot of $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}$ versus time $t$ results in a straight line with a slope equal to $\frac{k}{2.303}$ (Option A).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which will be the unit of rate constant for the reaction having $\text{Rate} = K[\text{A}]^{1/2}.[\text{B}]^{3/2}$?
questions.explanation: The overall order of the reaction is $n = 1/2 + 3/2 = 2$. The unit of the rate constant $k$ for a second-order reaction is $(\text{mol L}^{-1})^{1-n} \text{s}^{-1}$, which results in $\text{mol}^{-1} \text{L s}^{-1}$ (Option C).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: A reaction is first order in terms of A and second order in terms of B. What will be the rate of reaction, if concentration of B is increased two times?
questions.explanation: The rate law is $\text{Rate} = k[A]^1[B]^2$. If the concentration of $B$ is doubled while $A$ remains constant, the rate becomes $k[A](2[B])^2 = 4k[A][B]^2$, which is 4 times the original rate (Option A).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: For any reaction the rate constant $\text{K} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}^{-3/2} \text{ L}^{3/2} \text{S}^{-1}$; then the order of reaction will be ______.
questions.explanation: The unit of the rate constant $k$ is $(\text{mol L}^{-1})^{1-n} \text{s}^{-1}$. By equating the power of concentration $1-n$ to $-3/2$ (from $\text{mol}^{-3/2} \text{L}^{3/2}$), we find $n = 1 + 3/2 = 2.5$ (Option D).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following statements is incorrect for a reaction carried out in presence of catalyst?

options.A: Potential energy of reactants and products change
options.B: Equilibrium constant of the reaction does not change
options.C: There is no change in Gibbs energy of the reaction
options.D: The activation energy of the reaction decreases

questions.explanation: A catalyst lowers the activation energy ($E_a$) by providing an alternative pathway but does not change the potential energy of the reactants or products, making statement A incorrect. It also does not affect the Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$) or the equilibrium constant ($K_{eq}$).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Which of the following graphs is correct for a first order reaction $\text{R} ightarrow \text{P}$?
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, the integrated rate law is $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]} = \frac{k}{2.303} t$, which represents a linear relationship between $\log \frac{[R]_0}{[R]}$ and time (Option A). Other options are incorrect because the half-life $t_{1/2}$ is independent of the initial concentration for first-order reactions.
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Reaction $2\text{A} ightarrow \text{B} + 3\text{C}$ is zero order reaction. If $\text{K} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ S}^{-1}$; What will be the rate of production of 'C'?
questions.explanation: For a zero-order reaction, the rate is equal to the rate constant $k$. Given the stoichiometry $2A \rightarrow B + 3C$, the rate of production of $C$ is $3 \times \text{Rate} = 3 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 10.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$ (Option B).
questions.subject: chemistry

questions.question: Half life period for certain zero order reaction is $10\text{ min}$. then how much time is required for this reaction to complete $100\%$?
questions.explanation: In a zero-order reaction, the rate of reaction is constant, so the time required for $100\%$ completion is twice the half-life. Given $t_{1/2} = 10 \text{ min}$, the total time for the reaction to complete is $2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ min}$ (Option A).
questions.subject: chemistry
